papers.title: Simplified criteria to assess flange climbing derailment | Railway Engineering Science | Springer Nature Link
papers.abstract: Weinstock proposed a derailment criterion that considers the influence of both wheels on derailment, as opposed to focussing solely on the wheel susceptible to climbing. The logic of this derailment criterion is that when the FW begins to climb, the NFW, if connected by a rigid axle, will experience a downward sliding motion. This implies that the force required for one wheel to climb must overcome the resistive forces at both wheel–rail contact points. To derive this equation, both contact scenarios shown in Fig. 1 were analysed. The condition for the right wheel to climb is the same as Nadal

papers.title: On the investigation of wheel flange climb derailment mechanism and methods to control it | Molatefi | Journal of Theoretical and Applied Mechanics
papers.abstract:
Habibollah Molatefi, Amin Mazraeh

In this research, investigations are focused on the study of railway vehicle wheel flange climb derailment. A three dimensional nonlinear dynamic model of the wheel-set and suspension system is developed. Having validated the model through field tests, the effect of friction coefficient, wheel-set AOA, vehicle suspension system and running speed on the wheel flange climb derailment are investigated. In addition, different rail lubrication methods are studied and their effects on the wheel flange climb derailment are compared in the case of two point contact f

papers.title: A novel method to estimate derailment probability due to track geometric irregularities using reliability techniques and advanced simulation methods | Archive of Applied Mechanics | Springer Nature Link
papers.abstract: Track irregularities have a dramatic impact on the response and vibration of a railway vehicle and on the interaction between wheel and rail. The random nature of the track structure and constituent materials and the effects of other factors such as maintenance conditions and transit traffic give rise to the random nature of track irregularities. This research provides a method to estimate the derailment probability of a railway vehicle where track irregularities are assumed to be random, and the interaction of the track and the moving train is considered using advanced dynamic analysis. papers.title: Using Derailment Findings to Identify Derailment Risks – Interface Journal
papers.abstract:
By Bob Tuzik

History has shown that the potential for track buckling represents a derailment risk in CWR territory. Longitudinal forces, which can be generated from multiple sources, but, especially, from thermal expansion, increase the risk of track buckling, particularly on hot days. If the track components designed to restrain longitudinal movement of the rail are unable to resist the dynamic longitudinal and lateral forces that are applied by passing trains, thermal misalignment or track buckles occur. papers.title: Wheel–rail-induced derailment analysis - VTechWorks
papers.abstract: This paper offers a comprehensive review of the literature on the role of wheel–rail contact mechanics in train derailments. Using experimental and simulation methods, this review examines various research efforts that analyze how contact forces influence wheel climb and derailment dynamics. The related studies are summarized, and insights are provided on how they have contributed to understanding derailments and enhancing overall rolling stock safety. The review shows significant progress across different specialized areas within the broader topic of derailments. This includes advanced, state

papers.title: [PDF] How Accident Investigation Can Influence Railway Technology
papers.abstract: The paper includes four case studies based on the RAIB’s experiences which demonstrate how accident investigation can positively influence railway technology to bring about safety improvements. The case studies cover improving the integrity of critical final drive gearbox bearings, reducing freight train derailments, controlling post-derailment vehicle behaviour, and improving the survivability of passengers in high speed derailments. Keywords: railway accidents, investigation, railway technology, derailments. 1 Introduction Throughout railway history, lessons learned from accidents have drive

papers.title: Train Derailments | Causes & Prevention
papers.abstract:
## What are railroads doing to prevent train derailments?

Freight railroads—the safest way to move goods over land—are continuously working to reduce train derailments, especially on mainline tracks where trains travel long distances at higher speeds.

Railroads take a targeted, data-driven approach to safety investments, focusing on the most common causes of derailments: track defects and equipment failures. To address these risks, freight railroads invest billions annually in infrastructure maintenance, track upgrades, and rolling stock improvements. They also deploy advanced inspection tec

papers.title: Rail Vehicle Dynamics Simulation
papers.abstract:
##### Simpack – MBS Application for All Types of Rail System

Multibody system simulation (MBS) is a powerful tool for analysing and designing a wide range of rail-based or guided vehicles and mechanisms, including everything from tram cars to fully articulated high-speed trains. It is also applicable to specialized systems like roller coasters, material handling equipment, and even maglev trains. Simpack, the leading MBS software for railway system dynamics, is widely utilized by manufacturers and operators around the globe.

Typical applications include:

 Critical speed calculations
 Derail

papers.title: Research on the safety factor against derailment of railway vehicless
papers.abstract:
The study has specified the safety factor – the Nadal criterion – against the derailment of the wheels of a railway stock. Unlike in the traditional approach, the safety factor of the wheel steadiness against derailment is assessed taking the frame force, rather than the lateral load on the flange, as the main factor of safety. This approach to determining the stability criterion gives more reliable results, since the frame force is more accessible for experimental and theoretical analysis.

# Rail Vehicle Dynamics Simulation

Understanding and Optimizing Rail-Guided Systems

## Simulate Trains and Railway Dynamics

### Understanding Rail Vehicle Dynamics through MBS

Rail vehicles are complex systems and their performance is determined by the interaction of multiple components, including the rail-wheel-contact interface, suspension, bogies, couplings and other moving parts. Optimizing and understanding these interactions is essential to ensuring vehicle safety, speed, reliability and comfort. The complexity and sheer length of

Insufficient digital twin models for real-time track monitoring
Lack of climate-change impact studies on track geometry
Limited sensor-fusion approaches for early derailment warning
Sparse research on derailment risk in mixed-speed traffic corridors

key_findings: Derailment safety involves understanding wheel-rail contact mechanics and analyzing derailment causes; effective measures like guardrails are studied to prevent impacts and mitigate consequences
Track geometry irregularities significantly impact railway safety and derailment risk; peer-reviewed research emphasizes the importance of precise monitoring and maintenance to ensure safe operations
Derailment probability modeling uses statistical analysis and reliability techniques to estimate risks from track irregularities and operational factors
Peer-reviewed research focuses on advanced simulation methods and statistical models to predict and mitigate derailments
Key factors include track geometry, vehicle dynamics, and operational conditions
High-speed rail dynamics simulations focus on post-derailment behavior and safety measures
Research includes multibody models and derailment containment devices
Peer-reviewed studies analyze derailment causes and mitigation strategies
Railway bogie dynamics focus on lateral stability and derailment safety; peer-reviewed research emphasizes track-worthiness criteria and optimal maintenance strategies
Key factors include friction forces and dynamic load ratios
